# UC-22.2.1 · NIS2 Art.23(4)(a) — 24-Hour Early-Warning Notification Readiness

> **Criticality:** Critical &middot; **Difficulty:** Advanced &middot; **Pillar:** Security &middot; **Type:** Compliance, Security &middot; **Wave:** Crawl &middot; **Status:** Verified

*We watch every serious cyber alert and start a 24-hour clock the moment it is raised, so the team can warn the regulator before the clock runs out — not after, when it is too late.*

---

## Description

Detects ES notables raised at high or critical urgency that map to a NIS2-significant incident threshold and are approaching or have breached the Art.23(4)(a) 24-hour early-warning clock measured from notable creation, after excluding any time-bounded approved exceptions.

## Value

Missing the 24-hour early-warning window forfeits the supervisory authority's good-faith presumption under Art.32(4)(a) and triggers the Annex II inquiry obligations. This UC gives the Head of IR an auditable clock per significant incident so the early-warning is drafted before the deadline, not reconstructed afterwards.

## Implementation

(1) Onboard the `nis2_significant_incident_thresholds.csv` lookup mapping each correlation `rule_name` to its NIS2 tier (Annex I/II/Implementing Reg 2024/2690 thresholds). (2) Schedule the SPL every 15 minutes over the last 3 days. (3) Configure `evidence_id` summary indexing to `audit_evidence`. (4) Send PagerDuty page on `clock_state=WARN_T_MINUS_4H`, escalate on `BREACH`. (5) Throttle by `rule_name` for 4 hours so a single noisy rule does not re-page during one campaign.

## Evidence

Saved search `UC-22.2.1` row-per-finding output, `index=audit_evidence sourcetype=evidence:saved_search` archive (7-year retention, RFC 3161 TSA-signed via `evidenceSigning`), and the `NIS2 Art.23 — 24h clock` panel on dashboard `compliance/nis2-art23-posture.xml`. Daily PDF digest delivered to `head-of-ir@<entity>` and `csirt-liaison@<entity>` archived to the same evidence index.

## Control test

### Positive scenario

Fixture `sample-data/uc-22.2.1-fixture.json` injects a notable with `rule_name="NIS2-Significant-Ransomware"`, `urgency="critical"`, `status="In Progress"`, `_time=now()-21h`, lookup match `nis2_significant="yes"`, no exception. The search returns one row with `clock_state="WARN_T_MINUS_4H"`, `hours_since_detection`≈21, and a populated `evidence_id`.

### Negative scenario

Same fixture but with `status="Closed"` and a closure timestamp at `now()-19h`, OR with a non-expired entry in `nis2_exception_register.csv` for the rule_name. The search returns 0 rows. Verifies that closed notables and time-bound approved exceptions correctly suppress.

## Detailed Implementation

## Prerequisites

- **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
- **Lookups**: `nis2_significant_incident_thresholds.csv`, `nis2_exception_register.csv` must be deployed to the search-tier app and refreshed on a documented schedule. For KV Store lookups, verify with `| rest /servicesNS/-/-/data/transforms/lookups | search name IN ('nis2_significant_incident_thresholds.csv','nis2_exception_register.csv')`.
- **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `Art.23`. The control objective is: "Contributes evidence toward the Art.23 incident-reporting lifecycle by monitoring the 24-hour early-warning clock for significant incidents." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is bespoke for this UC — no single canonical TA parses every source the detection relies on. Route data into the relevant indexes under the relevant sourcetypes, and document the ingestion path in the UC runbook so future engineers know where each field originates.


5 operational considerations apply to the rollout of this detection:


- **Hint 1:** Onboard the `nis2_significant_incident_thresholds.csv` lookup mapping each correlation `rule_name` to its NIS2 tier (Annex I/II/Implementing Reg 2024/2690 thresholds). This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

- **Hint 2:** Schedule the SPL every 15 minutes over the last 3 days. Schedule cadence is a tradeoff between regulatory latency and search-head load; document the chosen cadence and the reasoning in the UC runbook.

- **Hint 3:** Configure `evidence_id` summary indexing to `audit_evidence`. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

- **Hint 4:** Send PagerDuty page on `clock_state=WARN_T_MINUS_4H`, escalate on `BREACH`. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

- **Hint 5:** Throttle by `rule_name` for 4 hours so a single noisy rule does not re-page during one campaign. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

For "NIS2 Art.23(4)(a) — 24-Hour Early-Warning Notification Readiness," the aggregation produces the result shape required by the NIS2 control — each row is a summary record the regulation expects to be presentable at audit time.

This detection correlates event arrival time with a business timestamp from `exception_valid_until` (format: `%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S`). The `strptime()` conversion to epoch is the linchpin — the subsequent `where` clause compares Splunk's `_time` against the parsed epoch to detect policy violations (activity after a termination, events past a retention boundary, operations outside a maintenance window, etc).

Silent failure mode: if `exception_valid_until` arrives in a format the `strptime` call doesn't match, the conversion returns null, propagates through the `where` comparison as 'unknown,' and the row is dropped from results. This produces under-reporting, not over-reporting — which is the dangerous direction for a compliance detection. Validate by running:

```spl
<base search>
| eval exception_valid_until_epoch = strptime(exception_valid_until, "%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S")
| where isnull(exception_valid_until_epoch)
| stats count by exception_valid_until
```

If this returns rows, the upstream system has multiple timestamp formats (common with SaaS connectors that change during version upgrades) and the `strptime` call must be hardened with `coalesce(strptime(exception_valid_until, "fmt1"), strptime(exception_valid_until, "fmt2"))` to handle them all.

Time-zone alignment: if the source system emits `exception_valid_until` in a different zone from the Splunk search head, the comparison will be offset. Look for `%z` or `%Z` in the format string — if absent, the conversion uses `TZ = TZ of Splunk host`, which is correct only when source and target agree. Search covers the last 3 days.

**CIM / accelerated variant:** The detection also has an accelerated variant using `| tstats` against the CIM `Alerts` datamodel:

```spl
| tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Alerts.Alerts where Alerts.severity IN ("high","critical") AND Alerts.status!="closed" AND _time >= relative_time(now(), "-3d") by Alerts.severity, Alerts.signature, Alerts.owner, Alerts._time
| rename Alerts.* as *
| eval hours_since_detection = round((now() - _time) / 3600, 2)
| where hours_since_detection >= 12
| sort - hours_since_detection
```

When to prefer this variant: once the underlying datamodel is accelerated (verify in Settings > Data Models > `Alerts` > status=100%), this query runs 10-50x faster than the raw variant and can feasibly cover multi-month windows for audit reporting. When to avoid: if your field extractions are app-specific and the CIM tagging is incomplete, the `tstats` variant will silently under-count. Always compare raw vs accelerated counts on a known day before swapping the production schedule. Maintain both variants in the UC — the raw is the primary for accuracy, the accelerated is the performance optimisation for dashboarding.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.2.1: NIS2 Art.23(4)(a) — 24-Hour Early-Warning Notification Readi]
cron_schedule = 0 */6 * * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -3d
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.2.1,reg=NIS2
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=the_index sourcetype=the_sourcetype earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `_time`, `rule_name`, `urgency`, `nis2_tier`, `status`, `owner`, `hours_since_detection`, `clock_state`, `src`, `dest`. Run:

```spl
index=the_index sourcetype=the_sourcetype earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table _time, rule_name, urgency, nis2_tier, status, owner, hours_since_detection, clock_state
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(c) Lookup freshness:** The detection joins against `nis2_significant_incident_thresholds.csv`. Check its modification time and row count:

```spl
| inputlookup nis2_significant_incident_thresholds.csv
| eval _lookup_row=1
| stats count as rows
```

Compare the row count to the expected baseline; an unexpected drop is a sign the upstream export truncated.

**(d) Threshold calibration:** Before enabling as a production alert, run the base search with the threshold removed and profile the distribution of `hours_since_detection`:

```spl
<base search> earliest=-30d
| stats perc50(hours_since_detection) as p50 perc90(hours_since_detection) as p90 perc99(hours_since_detection) as p99 max(hours_since_detection) as hi
```

The current threshold `24` should sit between p90 and p99 for a balanced signal-to-noise ratio. If it's below p50, expect heavy alert volume; if above p99, expect under-detection.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.2.1: NIS2 evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Row 1: Single-Value Tile `Significant Incidents In Clock Window` (Red ≥1). Row 2: Timeline Panel Showing Notable Aging With Three Threshold Bands (12H, 20H, 24H). Row 3: Sortable Table (Rule Name, Urgency, Owner, Hours Since Detection, Status, Drilldown To Es Incident Review For The Notable). Row 4: 14-Day Trellis Of `Clock State` Counts Per `Nis2 Tier` For Capacity-Review Meetings (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**Silent under-reporting:** The `strptime` conversion is likely failing on a subset of events — see Step 3 for the hardening procedure. Check for format drift after upstream system upgrades.

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**NIS2-specific failure modes:**

- 24/7 reporting obligation (Art. 23): NIS2 requires early warning within 24 hours of awareness of a significant incident. Detections that back this SLA must compute elapsed time from the SOC's first-acknowledged timestamp (not the resolve-time) and must trigger independently of business hours — the regulatory clock does not pause overnight.

- Member-state variation: NIS2 is transposed individually by each EU Member State with some latitude on thresholds and sector scoping. A detection correct for German IT-SiG 3 (BSI) may be misaligned with Dutch CWV or French LPM. Use `nis2_member_state_config.csv` to parametrize thresholds per jurisdiction; never hardcode.

- Essential vs Important entity criteria: the applicable obligations depend on sector + size. Before alerting, verify the monitored entity's designation; a detection firing on an out-of-scope subsidiary causes unnecessary regulatory notifications that erode trust with the national CSIRT.

- Supply-chain (Art. 21(2)(d)) extent: NIS2 explicitly includes direct suppliers' cybersecurity posture. Detections must cover monitored-by-you-not-owned-by-you infrastructure (outsourced SaaS, managed SIEM tenants) — these are in scope even if operationally run by a third party. BA/BAA-style governance applies.

## SPL

```spl
`notable` urgency IN ("high","critical") earliest=-3d
| eval hours_since_detection = round((now() - _time) / 3600, 2)
| lookup nis2_significant_incident_thresholds.csv rule_name OUTPUT nis2_tier nis2_significant
| eval nis2_significant = coalesce(nis2_significant, "unclassified")
| where nis2_significant != "no" AND status != "Closed"
| eval clock_state = case(
    hours_since_detection >= 24, "BREACH",
    hours_since_detection >= 20, "WARN_T_MINUS_4H",
    hours_since_detection >= 12, "NOTICE_T_MINUS_12H",
    1==1, "OK")
| where clock_state != "OK"
| lookup nis2_exception_register.csv rule_name OUTPUT exception_valid_until
| where isnull(exception_valid_until) OR strptime(exception_valid_until, "%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S") < now()
| eval evidence_id = sha256(rule_name . event_id . _time)
| eval owner = coalesce(owner, "UNASSIGNED")
| table _time, rule_name, urgency, nis2_tier, status, owner, hours_since_detection, clock_state, src, dest, risk_object, evidence_id
| sort - hours_since_detection
```

## CIM SPL

```spl
| tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Alerts.Alerts where Alerts.severity IN ("high","critical") AND Alerts.status!="closed" AND _time >= relative_time(now(), "-3d") by Alerts.severity, Alerts.signature, Alerts.owner, Alerts._time
| rename Alerts.* as *
| eval hours_since_detection = round((now() - _time) / 3600, 2)
| where hours_since_detection >= 12
| sort - hours_since_detection
```

## Visualization

Row 1: Single-value tile `Significant incidents in clock window` (red ≥1). Row 2: Timeline panel showing notable aging with three threshold bands (12h, 20h, 24h). Row 3: Sortable table (rule_name, urgency, owner, hours_since_detection, status, drilldown to ES Incident Review for the notable). Row 4: 14-day trellis of `clock_state` counts per `nis2_tier` for capacity-review meetings.

## Known False Positives

1) **Active major incident with declared crisis state** — analysts deliberately leave many notables open while triaging the underlying campaign. Distinguish: an open ServiceNow MAJ ticket and `risk_score > 250` on the parent risk object in `index=risk` for the same time window. Suppress: open a single time-bound exception in `nis2_exception_register.csv` keyed on the parent campaign rule_name for the duration of the declared crisis; do not suppress the rule globally. 2) **ES Risk-Based Alerting consolidating individual rule fires into an aggregate notable** — per-rule notables disappear and only the aggregate `Risk Analysis` notable fires. Distinguish: `urgency=informational` on the per-rule notable but `index=risk` shows recent contributions for that rule_name. Suppress: pivot the SPL to count risk-aggregated notables instead via the `Risk` data model. 3) **Bulk re-tuning after correlation-search release** — analysts re-triage while the rule's `lastModified` is recent. Distinguish: `| rest /servicesNS/-/SA-ThreatIntelligence/saved/searches | search title=<rule_name> | table updated`. Suppress: 7-day time-bound exception in `nis2_exception_register.csv`. 4) **Holiday weekend / unfilled on-call shift** — no analyst has acknowledged. Distinguish: cross-check `index=pagerduty sourcetype=pagerduty:incident` for an unacknowledged page tied to the notable. Do NOT suppress — the aged notable is a legitimate IR-drill finding and should escalate the on-call schedule. 5) **Notable raised via the `Risk Analysis` correlation search at urgency=informational** — collected for context, not paged. Distinguish: `urgency="informational"`. Suppress: filter `urgency IN ("high","critical")` in the SPL (already enforced). 6) **Forwarder clock skew on the indexer that received the notable** — `_time` is hours behind real time. Distinguish: `| eval lag=_indextime-_time | where lag > 1800` returns rows. Investigate via `chronyc sources` on the source. Suppress: use `_indextime` instead of `_time` for the clock calculation only when forwarder skew is documented in `nis2_known_skew_hosts.csv`.
